mappings.rationaleAI: Death as the irreparable disruption of life processes (A), covering clinical definitions, causes including aging and disease, and evolutionary aspects of mortality.

Disease as a departure from homeostasis (A), encompassing noncommunicable metabolic and environmental diseases (A.1), communicable contagious diseases (A.2), and control through prevention and treatment (A.4).
Asthma as a chronic noncommunicable respiratory disease with environmental triggers (A.1), also relevant as an animal disease when affecting domesticated animals (C).
Heart attack as a noncommunicable disease caused by atherosclerotic metabolic processes and environmental risk factors (A.1).
Smoking as an environmental hazard causing noncommunicable diseases including lung cancer, cardiovascular disease, and COPD (A.1).
Physiology bridging biology and medicine, with physiological state denoting normal function and deviations constituting disease (A).
Pasteur co-founded germ theory establishing the basis for understanding contagious disease (A.2), developed vaccines conferring immunity (A.3), and advanced disease control and prevention (A.4).
Infection as pathogen invasion and multiplication causing communicable disease (A.2), with multifactorial concepts including host susceptibility and environmental conditions (A.2.a).
The common cold as the most frequent human infectious communicable disease (A.2) with endemic seasonal patterns (A.2.b).
Influenza as a communicable respiratory disease (A.2) causing seasonal epidemics and periodic pandemics (A.2.b).
Ageing as a cumulative biological process causing noncommunicable metabolic dysfunction and accounting for two-thirds of daily global deaths (A.1).
Health as the normal state from which disease represents a departure or disruption of homeostasis (A).
Mental health disorders as noncommunicable diseases encompassing biological, demographic, and environmental risk factors (A.1).
Hygiene practices maintaining health and preventing disease through infection control (A.4).
Nutritional deficiencies and excesses producing malnutrition as noncommunicable metabolic defects (A.1).
Radioactive decay posing biological hazard as an environmental hazard causing noncommunicable disease (A.1).
Innate and adaptive immunity as the biological basis of disease immunity (A.3), including vaccination as the basis for acquired immune protection.
Domesticated animals susceptible to managed and natural diseases, relevant to animal diseases (C).
Life defined by homeostasis and metabolism, with disease representing disruption of these vital processes (A).
Famine producing regional malnutrition and epidemic disease as an environmental hazard causing noncommunicable disease (A.1).
Violence resulting in injury and death as an environmental hazard disrupting homeostasis (A.1).
Programmed cell death and cellular dysfunction underlying disease processes at the fundamental level of life organization (A).
Brain disorders including neurodegeneration as examples of animal diseases affecting nervous system function (C).
